content.plain_english: This week your child is counting and writing numbers up to 50. They are learning to break a number into tens and ones — so 47 is four tens and seven ones.
content.in_school: Children build numbers with bundles of ten straws or base-ten blocks. They count in 2s and 5s up to 50, place numbers on a long number line, and find one more or one less than any number.
content.home_activity: Find any pile of 30-50 small items at home — buttons, pasta, Lego bricks. Ask your child to make piles of ten until they run out. How many tens? How many left over? That's the number. Two minutes — keep it playful.
dinner_table_questions: Can you count in 2s from 20 to 50?
What number is one less than 30?

key_vocabulary.definition: Single items left after making tens.
Step up by five each time — 5, 10, 15, 20.
What each digit stands for, depending on its position.

example_questions: How many tens are in 38? How many ones?
content.times_table_tip: Year 1 children practise counting in 2s, 5s and 10s. Count in 5s while drying hands — by 'twenty-five' you've counted to where five fingers (twice) would take you.
